term: ERTR (Electronically Recorded Transferable Right / 電子記録移転権利)
plain_definition: Japan's security-token category. Under the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act, certain instruments that would otherwise get lighter 'second-paragraph' securities treatment -- such as a trust beneficial interest or a collective investment scheme interest, the structures typically used for real-estate and fund tokenization -- are automatically upgraded to full, first-paragraph securities treatment (the same disclosure and business-conduct rules that apply to listed shares or corporate bonds) whenever they are tokenized on a system that allows electronic transfer, unless a narrow low-circulation carve-out applies. The policy logic: tokenizing an instrument makes it materially easier to trade, so the law responds by upgrading its regulatory treatment to match ordinary, freely tradable securities.
formal_definition: Financial Instruments and Exchange Act (金融商品取引法, Act No. 25 of 1948, promulgated 13 April 1948), Art. 2(3), inserted by Act No. 28 of 2019 (令和元年法律第28号), effective 1 May 2020 under Cabinet Order No. 141 of 3 April 2020. Art. 2(3) reclassifies an Art. 2(2) deemed security as an 'electronically recorded transferable right' (電子記録移転権利, ERTR) -- and therefore an Art. 2(1) ('first-paragraph') security -- whenever it is displayed as property value transferable using an electronic data processing system, unless a Cabinet Office Ordinance carve-out for low-circulation cases applies. Once an instrument is an ERTR, a public offering or secondary distribution requires a securities registration statement under Art. 4(1), and dealing in or brokering it requires registration as a Type I Financial Instruments Business Operator under Art. 29 -- the same registration tier used for ordinary broker-dealers. Confirmed by direct fetch of the current consolidated FIEA text via the e-Gov Law API this session (law_id 323AC0000000025).
